title: Exceptions & Secure Exception Handling
description: Master Java exceptions and learn how poor exception handling creates critical security vulnerabilities including authentication bypass, information leakage, and denial of service attacks.
learning_outcomes: Understand the Java exception hierarchy and the difference between checked and unchecked exceptions
Identify security vulnerabilities caused by poor exception handling including authentication bypass
Explain how information leakage occurs through verbose error messages and stack traces
Implement secure exception handling patterns that prevent denial of service attacks
Apply the 'catch or declare' requirement correctly for checked exceptions
Use try-catch-finally blocks to ensure proper resource cleanup
Understand CWE-395, CWE-476, and CWE-200 and their real-world impact
Implement secure logging that separates sensitive data from public error messages
Design exception handling for long-lived services that maintains availability
Apply OWASP secure design principles to exception handling code

explanation_styles.analogy: Think of exceptions like fire alarms in a building. If the alarm goes off (exception thrown), you need a plan (catch block). If you ignore the alarm (empty catch), the building could burn down (security breach). And you definitely don't want the alarm to broadcast your home address to everyone (information leakage)!
explanation_styles.visual: Picture a flow: Normal Code → Something Goes Wrong → Exception Thrown → catch Block Handles It → Program Continues Safely. If ANY step is missing, attackers can exploit the gap.
explanation_styles.step_by_step: Step 1: Identify what could go wrong. Step 2: Wrap risky code in try. Step 3: Catch SPECIFIC exceptions first. Step 4: Always set safe defaults in catch. Step 5: Never expose internal details in error messages. Step 6: Clean up resources in finally.
explanation_styles.code_trace: Let me trace through this code line by line, showing you exactly what value each variable holds and what happens when the exception is triggered...
explanation_styles.comparison: UNSAFE: catch(Exception e) { } ← empty catch, attacker wins! SAFE: catch(Exception e) { loggedIn = false; log.error(e); } ← fail securely, log privately.

theory.introduction:
Imagine you're a pilot flying a plane. During the flight, dozens of things could go wrong - engine failure, bad weather, instrument malfunction. A good pilot doesn't just hope nothing goes wrong; they have specific procedures for EVERY possible emergency. In programming, exceptions are those emergencies - unexpected situations that interrupt the normal flow of your program.

But here's the critical security angle: HOW you handle those emergencies can make the difference between a safe landing and a catastrophe. In the world of software security, poor exception handling has led to some of the most devastating attacks in history. The Ariane 5 rocket explosion in 1996 was caused by an unhandled exception (an integer overflow that caused a 64-bit to 16-bit conversion error), destroying a $370 million rocket in just 37 seconds after launch.

In this course, you'll learn not just how Java exceptions work, but how attackers exploit poor exception handling to bypass authentication, steal sensitive information, and crash entire systems. You'll discover that a single empty catch block can be more dangerous than having no security at all - because it gives you a FALSE sense of safety while leaving the door wide open for attackers.

This is one of the most practical and immediately applicable security skills you can learn. Every code review by security professionals checks exception handling first, because it's where the most common and most exploitable vulnerabilities hide.
theory.why_important: Exception handling vulnerabilities are listed in multiple CWE categories including CWE-395 (Use of NullPointerException catch to detect NULL pointer dereference), CWE-476 (NULL Pointer Dereference), and CWE-200 (Exposure of Sensitive Information). Poor exception handling can lead to authentication bypass (logging in without a password!), denial of service (crashing an entire server), and information leakage (revealing database structures, passwords, and internal paths to attackers). In the OWASP Top 10, improper error handling contributes to multiple categories. Every professional security audit begins by examining how code handles exceptions - it's often the fastest way to find exploitable vulnerabilities.

key_concepts.explanation: An exception is an event that disrupts the normal flow of a program's execution. Think of it like a speed bump on a road - your car (program) is driving along smoothly, then suddenly hits a bump (exception). If you're prepared (have exception handling), you slow down and continue. If you're not prepared, you might damage your car (program crashes). In Java, exceptions are objects - they are instances of classes that extend the Throwable class. When something goes wrong, Java 'throws' an exception object that contains information about what went wrong, including a message and a stack trace showing where it happened.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Think of exceptions like a safety net for trapeze artists. Without the net (try-catch), one slip means disaster. With the net, you catch the fall and can try again safely!

key_concepts.explanation: Java organises exceptions in a family tree (class hierarchy). At the top is Throwable, which has two children: Error (serious problems like running out of memory - usually can't recover) and Exception (problems your code should handle). Exception has a special child called RuntimeException - these are 'unchecked' exceptions that happen due to programming mistakes like NullPointerException or ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException. All other exceptions under Exception are 'checked' - the compiler FORCES you to handle them. Understanding this hierarchy is crucial for security because different types of exceptions require different handling strategies.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Think of checked exceptions like mandatory seatbelts - Java won't let you drive without them. Unchecked exceptions are like potholes - you SHOULD watch for them, but nobody forces you to.

key_concepts.explanation: This is one of Java's most important distinctions, and it has major security implications. CHECKED exceptions extend Exception (but NOT RuntimeException) - the compiler forces you to either catch them with try-catch OR declare them with 'throws' in your method signature. Examples: FileNotFoundException, IOException, SQLException. UNCHECKED exceptions extend RuntimeException - the compiler does NOT force you to handle them. Examples: NullPointerException, ArithmeticException, ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException. The security danger: because unchecked exceptions don't require handling, programmers often IGNORE them. But attackers know this and deliberately trigger unchecked exceptions to exploit unhandled error paths! Remember the mnemonic: rUNtime = UNchecked.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Checked exceptions are like mandatory fire drills - you MUST have a plan. Unchecked exceptions are like earthquakes - no one forces you to prepare, but you'd be foolish not to!

key_concepts.explanation: The try-catch-finally block is Java's primary tool for handling exceptions. The TRY block contains code that might throw an exception. The CATCH block handles the exception if one occurs - you can have multiple catch blocks for different exception types. The FINALLY block runs NO MATTER WHAT - whether an exception occurred or not. This is critical for security because finally ensures cleanup code always runs (closing files, database connections, releasing locks). Without finally, a thrown exception could leave resources open, creating vulnerabilities. The order matters: catch specific exceptions FIRST, then more general ones.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Think of finally like a cleanup crew after a party - whether the party went well (no exception) or went badly (exception thrown), the cleanup crew ALWAYS comes to tidy up!

key_concepts.concept: SECURITY VULNERABILITY: Authentication Bypass via Exceptions
key_concepts.explanation: This is one of the most dangerous and common exception vulnerabilities. Consider a login function where the loggedIn flag starts as TRUE and is only set to FALSE if the password doesn't match. If an exception is thrown BEFORE the password check (for example, by passing null as the password, which triggers a NullPointerException), the catch block might do NOTHING (empty catch), and the function returns the initial TRUE value - granting access WITHOUT a valid password! This exact vulnerability pattern has been found in real banking systems and authentication modules. The fix is simple but critical: ALWAYS set the default to the SAFE state (loggedIn = false) and only change it on successful verification.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Imagine a nightclub bouncer who lets EVERYONE in by default and only stops people whose name ISN'T on the list. If the list blows away in the wind (exception), everyone gets in! The secure approach: deny by default, only allow verified guests.

key_concepts.concept: SECURITY VULNERABILITY: Information Leakage Through Error Messages
key_concepts.explanation: When an application displays detailed error messages, stack traces, or debug information to users, it gives attackers a roadmap of the system's internals. A stack trace reveals class names, method names, line numbers, file paths, database structure, and sometimes even credentials. In production, applications should NEVER display stack traces or internal error details to users. Instead, show a generic error message and log the details privately. The CWE-200 (Exposure of Sensitive Information to an Unauthorized Actor) directly covers this vulnerability. Attackers use this information to understand the application's architecture and find more vulnerabilities to exploit.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Imagine if every time your house alarm went off, it announced to the whole neighborhood: 'The back door is unlocked, the safe is in the bedroom, and the code is 1234!' That's what verbose error messages do for attackers.

key_concepts.concept: SECURITY VULNERABILITY: Denial of Service via Unhandled Exceptions
key_concepts.explanation: If your code does not include exception handling, a single error could cause a crash, potentially bringing down the entire system. Attackers exploit this by deliberately sending inputs that trigger unhandled exceptions. This is a Denial of Service (DoS) attack - the attacker doesn't need to break IN, they just need to break the service so legitimate users can't use it. For web servers and long-lived services, this is especially critical because one unhandled exception can terminate the entire server process, affecting ALL users. The mitigation is defence in depth: handle exceptions at every level, with a catch-all at the highest level that prevents total system failure.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Think of a restaurant kitchen. If ONE dish goes wrong and there's no plan, the chef panics and shuts down the ENTIRE kitchen - nobody else gets fed either. That's DoS via unhandled exceptions!

key_concepts.concept: Secure Logging: Separating Public and Private Error Information
key_concepts.explanation: The ideal approach is to never log sensitive data at all, but for debugging purposes this isn't always feasible. The practical solution is to establish a secondary, secure logging repository. When an error occurs, store the full details (including stack trace, variable values, and context) in a protected log that only administrators can access. Give the user a generic error message plus a reference ID they can quote to support. The reference ID should be unpredictable and arbitrary - using sequential numbers could reveal to attackers how frequently errors occur, which helps them understand the system's patterns. Use Java's java.util.logging.Logger or frameworks like Log4j for proper logging with access controls.
key_concepts.ai_hint: It's like a hospital: the patient (user) gets told 'You have an infection' (generic message). The detailed lab results with exact bacteria counts and medications (sensitive details) go into the medical file that only doctors (admins) can read, along with a case number (reference ID).

key_concepts.concept: CWE-395: Using NullPointerException Catch to Detect NULL
key_concepts.explanation: CWE-395 describes the anti-pattern of catching NullPointerException to detect null values instead of using explicit null checks. This is dangerous because: (1) NullPointerException can occur at multiple points in a try block, making it ambiguous which variable was null, (2) catching the exception is MUCH slower than a simple null check, (3) it hides the real location of the bug, making debugging harder, and (4) it can mask other programming errors. The secure practice is to ALWAYS check for null BEFORE using a variable, not to rely on catching the NullPointerException after it happens. This is explicitly listed in the CWE vulnerability database and is a common finding in security audits.
key_concepts.ai_hint: It's like using a smoke alarm to figure out if you left the stove on. Sure, the alarm will go off eventually, but by then there might be a fire! It's much better to just CHECK the stove directly.

key_concepts.explanation: CWE-476 occurs when a program dereferences (tries to use) a pointer/reference that it expects to be valid but is actually NULL. In Java, this manifests as NullPointerException. This can cause program crashes (denial of service), but more dangerously, when combined with poor exception handling, it can lead to authentication bypass or security control bypass as we saw in the login example. Attackers deliberately pass null values to trigger this vulnerability. Prevention requires: (1) validating all inputs including checking for null, (2) never assuming method return values are non-null, (3) using Java's Optional class for values that might be absent, and (4) adding null checks before EVERY dereference of variables from external sources.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Imagine trying to open a door that doesn't exist. You reach for the handle (dereference), but there's nothing there (null) - you stumble and fall (crash). Always check if the door exists before trying to open it!

key_concepts.concept: Best Practices for Secure Exception Handling
key_concepts.explanation: Security experts follow these 10 rules for exception handling: (1) Use fail-safe defaults - always deny/false by default. (2) Catch specific exceptions before general ones. (3) NEVER leave catch blocks empty - at minimum, set safe defaults and log. (4) Don't use exception handling for normal flow control. (5) Don't expose internal details in error messages. (6) Log sensitive details to secure, access-controlled logs only. (7) Use finally for cleanup - always close resources. (8) Validate inputs BEFORE processing to prevent exceptions. (9) Don't log stack traces or configuration data publicly. (10) For long-lived services, add catch-all handlers at the highest level to prevent total failure. Following these rules prevents the three main exception-based attacks: authentication bypass, information leakage, and denial of service.
key_concepts.ai_hint: These 10 rules are like the pre-flight checklist pilots use before every takeoff. Skip even ONE step and you're risking disaster. Security professionals check these in every code review!

key_concepts.explanation: Java's compiler enforces a rule for checked exceptions: you must either CATCH the exception (handle it with try-catch) or DECLARE it (add 'throws' to your method signature to pass responsibility to the caller). This is Java's way of forcing you to think about what could go wrong. If you choose to declare (throws), you're saying 'I know this exception can happen, but I want my caller to deal with it.' This pushes the responsibility up the call stack until SOMEONE handles it. From a security perspective, declaring exceptions is fine as long as someone in the chain eventually handles them securely. But if no one handles them, the exception propagates to the JVM and terminates the program - a potential denial of service.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Think of it like a hot potato game. When Java throws a checked exception, SOMEONE must catch it. You can either catch it yourself (try-catch) or throw it to the next person (throws). But if nobody catches it, the game ends (program crashes)!

key_concepts.concept: OWASP Secure Design Principles Related to Exceptions
key_concepts.explanation: The OWASP Foundation defines secure design principles that directly relate to exception handling. FAIL SECURELY means that when something goes wrong, the system should default to a secure state (deny access, not grant it). DEFENCE IN DEPTH means having multiple layers of exception handling - at the method level, service level, and application level. LEAST PRIVILEGE means error messages should reveal the minimum information necessary. SEPARATION OF DUTIES means separating error reporting (to users) from error logging (to administrators). PSYCHOLOGICAL ACCEPTABILITY means security measures shouldn't be so cumbersome that developers skip them. These principles were established by Saltzer and Schroeder in 1975 and remain the foundation of all secure system design today.
key_concepts.ai_hint: OWASP principles are like the safety rules at a construction site: wear a hard hat (fail securely), have multiple safety rails (defence in depth), only share blueprints with authorised workers (least privilege), and make the rules easy to follow (psychological acceptability).

complete_examples.title: Example 1: Basic Try-Catch — Preventing a Crash
complete_examples.description: Shows how try-catch prevents a program crash from division by zero
complete_examples.output:
Program starting...
ERROR: Cannot divide by zero!
Exception type: ArithmeticException
Message: / by zero
Program continues running.
Other operations work fine.
complete_examples.ai_explanation: Line 1: We define our class. Line 4: The program starts normally. Lines 7-10: Inside the try block, we attempt division by zero. Line 10 would crash without try-catch! Line 11: This line NEVER executes because the exception is thrown at line 10. Lines 12-15: The catch block catches ArithmeticException. We print a friendly error message plus the exception details. Lines 18-19: Crucially, the program CONTINUES after the catch block. Without try-catch, lines 18-19 would never execute. The key lesson: try-catch is your safety net - it catches the fall and lets the program continue.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Try changing the denominator to a non-zero value. What happens? Then try catching Exception instead of ArithmeticException - does it still work? Why or why not?

complete_examples.title: Example 2: Multiple Catch Blocks — Handling Different Exceptions
complete_examples.description: Demonstrates catching multiple specific exceptions with different handling strategies
complete_examples.output:
Processing index 0...
Success! Number = 42

Processing index 1...
ERROR: 'hello' is not a valid number!

Processing index 2...
ERROR: Value at index 2 is null!

Processing index 3...
Success! Number = 99

Processing index 4...
ERROR: Index 4 is out of bounds!
Array only has 4 elements (0-3)

All items processed safely.
complete_examples.ai_explanation: This example processes an array with different types of problems. Index 0 ('42'): Parses successfully - no exception. Index 1 ('hello'): Integer.parseInt throws NumberFormatException - caught by the second catch block. Index 2 (null): Integer.parseInt(null) throws NullPointerException at the null.toString() call internally - caught by the third catch block. Index 3 ('99'): Parses successfully. Index 4: Array only has indices 0-3, so accessing index 4 throws ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException - caught by the first catch block. The key lesson: ORDER matters! Put specific exceptions BEFORE general ones. Each exception gets its own appropriate error message.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: What happens if you change the catch order and put a catch(Exception e) as the FIRST catch block? Why is this a bad practice for security?

complete_examples.title: Example 3: The Finally Block — Guaranteed Cleanup
complete_examples.description: Shows how finally ensures resource cleanup happens even when exceptions occur
complete_examples.output:
=== Test 1: No exception ===
Opening resource...
Calculating 10 / 2
Result = 5
FINALLY: Closing resource (always runs!)

=== Test 2: Exception occurs ===
Opening resource...
Calculating 10 / 0
CAUGHT: / by zero
FINALLY: Closing resource (always runs!)

=== Test 3: Null input ===
CAUGHT: Input was null!
FINALLY: Cleanup complete (always runs!)
complete_examples.ai_explanation: Test 1: No exception occurs. The try block completes normally, then finally runs. Test 2: ArithmeticException is thrown, caught by catch, then finally STILL runs. Test 3: NullPointerException from null.length(), caught, then finally runs. The critical observation: finally ALWAYS runs regardless of whether an exception occurred or not. This is essential for security because it ensures: database connections are closed, file handles are released, locks are freed, temporary files are deleted. Without finally, an exception could leave resources open - creating memory leaks and potential vulnerabilities.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Try removing the catch block and keeping only try-finally. What happens when an exception occurs? Does finally still run before the exception propagates?

complete_examples.title: Example 4: SECURITY — Authentication Bypass via Empty Catch Block
complete_examples.description: Demonstrates the exact login bypass vulnerability from security research - how an empty catch block allows authentication bypass
complete_examples.output:
=== VULNERABLE LOGIN ===
Normal login: false
ATTACK (null pwd): true

=== SECURE LOGIN ===
Normal login: false
  [LOG] Null input detected
ATTACK (null pwd): false
complete_examples.ai_explanation: THE ATTACK: The vulnerable login starts with loggedIn = true. When an attacker passes null as the password, pwd.getBytes() throws NullPointerException. The empty catch block does nothing. So loggedIn stays true and the attacker gets in WITHOUT knowing the password! THE FIX: The secure version (1) starts with loggedIn = false (fail-safe default), (2) checks for null inputs before processing, (3) explicitly sets loggedIn = false in the catch block. This is based on a REAL vulnerability pattern found in production banking systems. The lesson: NEVER start with a permissive default. NEVER leave catch blocks empty.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Try modifying the vulnerable version to also bypass when user is null. Then fix it in the secure version. What other values besides null could trigger unexpected exceptions?

complete_examples.title: Example 5: SECURITY — Information Leakage via Stack Traces
complete_examples.description: Shows how verbose error messages and stack traces expose sensitive system information to attackers
complete_examples.output:
=== Attacker tries wrong password ===

--- VULNERABLE Error Output ---
Error: Wrong password for user=admin entered=hacker123 expected=SuperSecret123
Stack trace:
java.lang.Exception: Wrong password for user=admin entered=hacker123 expected=SuperSecret123
	at InfoLeakDemo.vulnerableLogin(InfoLeakDemo.java:18)
	at InfoLeakDemo.main(InfoLeakDemo.java:48)

--- SECURE Error Output ---
  [ADMIN LOG] ERR-47283: Authentication failed user=admin
Login failed. Username or password incorrect.
Contact support with reference: ERR-47283
complete_examples.ai_explanation: THE VULNERABILITY: The vulnerable version leaks THREE critical pieces of information: (1) the actual username exists ('admin'), (2) the password the attacker tried, and (3) THE REAL PASSWORD ('SuperSecret123')! Plus the stack trace reveals class names, method names, and line numbers - a roadmap for the attacker. THE SECURE VERSION: (1) Doesn't distinguish between 'wrong user' and 'wrong password' (prevents username enumeration), (2) Shows only a generic 'Login failed' message, (3) Generates a random reference ID, (4) Logs details to a secure admin-only log. An attacker learns NOTHING useful from the secure version.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: The vulnerable version also leaks info when a user doesn't exist. Modify it to test with user='hacker' and see what information is revealed. Why does the secure version use the SAME error message for both cases?

complete_examples.title: Example 6: Checked vs Unchecked — The Compiler's Safety Net
complete_examples.description: Demonstrates the practical difference between checked and unchecked exceptions and why it matters for security
complete_examples.output:
=== Checked Exception ===
CHECKED: File not found - handled safely

=== Unchecked - DANGEROUS ===
CRASHED: NumberFormatException

=== Unchecked - SAFE ===
SAFE: 'abc' is not a number
SAFE: Cannot divide by zero
SAFE: Input cannot be null
SAFE Result: 20
complete_examples.ai_explanation: CHECKED exceptions (like FileNotFoundException) are your friend - the compiler FORCES you to handle them, so you can't accidentally forget. UNCHECKED exceptions (like NumberFormatException, NullPointerException) are sneaky - the compiler lets you ignore them, and they'll crash your program at runtime. The riskyCalculation method compiles fine but crashes with bad input. The safeCalculation method handles ALL possible problems. Security lesson: just because the compiler doesn't force you to handle an exception doesn't mean it's safe to ignore! Attackers specifically target unchecked exception paths because programmers often leave them unprotected.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: What happens if you remove the try-catch from main() around riskyCalculation('abc')? The program would crash entirely. Now imagine this is a web server handling 1000 users - one bad request takes down EVERYONE.

complete_examples.title: Example 7: SECURITY — Denial of Service via Unhandled Exceptions
complete_examples.description: Shows how an attacker can crash a service by sending inputs that trigger unhandled exceptions
complete_examples.output:
=== SECURE SERVER handles everything ===
Input '42': [200] Processing user #42
Input 'abc': [400] Bad request: not a number
Input '': [400] Bad request: empty input
Input 'null': [400] Bad request: empty input
Input '99999999999': [400] Bad request: not a number
Input '-1': [400] Bad request: ID out of range
Input '5': [200] Processing user #5

=== VULNERABLE SERVER crashes ===
Input '42': Processing user #42
Input 'abc': SERVER CRASHED: NumberFormatException
Input '': SERVER CRASHED: NumberFormatException
Input 'null': SERVER CRASHED: NullPointerException
Input '99999999999': SERVER CRASHED: NumberFormatException
Input '-1': Processing user #-1
Input '5': Processing user #5
complete_examples.ai_explanation: The vulnerable server crashes on 4 out of 7 inputs - that's a 57% crash rate! An attacker could send thousands of malicious requests to keep the server crashing repeatedly, preventing legitimate users from accessing the service. The secure server handles ALL inputs gracefully with appropriate error codes (400 for bad input, 500 for unexpected errors). It validates input, checks ranges, catches specific exceptions, and has a catch-all safety net. Note that the vulnerable server also accepts -1 as a valid user ID - another bug! The secure version rejects negative IDs with range validation.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Try adding more attack inputs like very long strings, special characters, or SQL injection attempts. Does the secure server handle them all? What about input like '1; DROP TABLE users'?

complete_examples.title: Example 8: Secure Logging with Reference IDs
complete_examples.description: Implements the recommended pattern of secure logging with unpredictable reference IDs
complete_examples.output:
=== User Experience ===
Payment of $50.0 processed.

Payment could not be processed.
Please try again or contact support.
Reference: a3f7b2c1

Payment could not be processed.
Please try again or contact support.
Reference: 8e4d9f26

=== Admin Log (restricted access) ===
[a3f7b2c1] Payment processing | Exception: java.lang.IllegalArgumentException | Message: Invalid card: null | Time: 2026-02-21T19:30:00
[8e4d9f26] Payment processing | Exception: java.lang.RuntimeException | Message: Payment gateway timeout for card ending 1111 | Time: 2026-02-21T19:30:00
complete_examples.ai_explanation: This demonstrates the recommended secure logging pattern. The USER sees: a generic 'could not process' message and a reference ID they can give to support. The ADMIN LOG sees: full exception details including the type, message, context, and timestamp. Notice: (1) The reference IDs are UUID-based and unpredictable - sequential IDs would reveal how often errors occur. (2) The user NEVER sees card numbers, exception types, or internal messages. (3) The admin log contains everything needed for debugging. (4) The successful payment reveals no unnecessary information either. This exact pattern is what security professionals recommend and is based on real-world security case studies.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: What if an attacker called processPayment thousands of times to fill up the log? How would you add rate limiting or log rotation to prevent this?

complete_examples.title: Example 9: CWE-395 — Wrong Way vs Right Way to Check for Null
complete_examples.description: Demonstrates why catching NullPointerException is an anti-pattern and how to properly check for null
complete_examples.output:
=== VULNERABLE (CWE-395) ===
User 0: ALICE <alice@mail.com>
User 1: Unknown user
User 2: Unknown user
User 3: Invalid user ID

=== SECURE (Explicit Checks) ===
User 0: ALICE <alice@mail.com>
  [LOG] Missing name for userId: 1
User 1: UNKNOWN <bob@mail.com>
  [LOG] Missing email for userId: 2
User 2: CHARLIE <no-email>
  [LOG] Invalid userId: 3
User 3: Invalid user ID
complete_examples.ai_explanation: THE PROBLEM WITH CWE-395: The vulnerable version catches NullPointerException as a blanket 'something was null' handler. For User 1, name is null. For User 2, email is null. But the catch block can't tell which was null - it returns the same 'Unknown user' for both, losing important context. THE SECURE APPROACH: Explicit null checks identify EXACTLY which field is null, log the specific issue, provide appropriate defaults, and continue processing the non-null fields. User 1 gets their email shown with a placeholder name. User 2 gets their name shown with a placeholder email. Much better user experience AND better security logging!
complete_examples.ai_challenge: What if you had a chain like user.getProfile().getAddress().getCity()? Write both the CWE-395 way (bad) and the explicit null check way (good) for this chain.

complete_examples.title: Example 10: Exception Handling in Long-Lived Services
complete_examples.description: Shows the recommended pattern for catch-all exception handling in services that must stay running
complete_examples.output:
=== Long-Lived Service Starting ===
Processing 10 requests...

Request 1: OK: Request 1 processed
Request 2: [400] Bad parameter
Request 3: [500] Internal error (NullPointerException)
Request 4: OK: Request 4 completed
Request 5: OK: Request 5 processed
Request 6: [500] Internal error (RuntimeException)
Request 7: OK: Request 7 completed
Request 8: [400] Bad parameter
Request 9: OK: Request 9 processed
Request 10: OK: Request 10 completed

=== Service Status ===
Processed: 10
Success: 7
Errors: 3
Service still running!
complete_examples.ai_explanation: This demonstrates the recommended pattern for long-lived services (web servers, APIs, background processors). Key principles: (1) LAYER 1: Try to process normally. (2) LAYER 2: Catch SPECIFIC, expected exceptions with appropriate error responses. (3) LAYER 3: CATCH-ALL with 'catch(Exception e)' as a safety net - this is normally bad practice, but for long-lived services it's ESSENTIAL to prevent total failure. Without the catch-all, a single NullPointerException or RuntimeException would terminate the entire service, affecting ALL users. The service processed all 10 requests even though 3 had errors. In production, the catch-all would trigger alerts and detailed logging.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: What about Errors (like OutOfMemoryError)? Should we catch those too? Research when it's appropriate to catch Throwable instead of Exception.

complete_examples.title: Example 11: Custom Exceptions for Security Events
complete_examples.description: Shows how to create custom exception classes for security-specific error handling
complete_examples.output:
Welcome, admin!
admin accessed /admin/users
Login failed.
  [AUDIT] User: hacker Reason: user not found
Login failed.
  [AUDIT] User:  Reason: empty username
Login failed.
  [AUDIT] User: admin Reason: wrong password
Account locked. Try again later.
  [AUDIT] Rate limit hit
complete_examples.ai_explanation: This demonstrates professional security exception design. Custom exceptions carry security-relevant data (who, what, why) that is only accessible through audit methods - NEVER through getMessage() which might be shown to users. Key features: (1) AuthenticationException stores the username and failure reason for audit logs. (2) AuthorisationException tracks who tried to access what resource. (3) RateLimitException prevents brute force attacks after 3 failed attempts. (4) The generic getMessage() returns safe messages like 'Authentication failed' or 'Access denied'. (5) The getAuditInfo() method provides detailed information only for logging. This separation ensures users never see sensitive details while admins get full visibility.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Add a BruteForceDetectionException that triggers when the same username fails 5 times. Store the IP address and timestamps of attempts. How would you implement account lockout?

complete_examples.title: Example 12: Try-With-Resources — Modern Secure Cleanup
complete_examples.description: Demonstrates Java's try-with-resources for automatic resource management
complete_examples.output:
=== Old Way ===
Hello, secure world!
Exception handling matters!

=== New Way (try-with-resources) ===
Hello, secure world!
Exception handling matters!

=== Nonexistent file ===
File not found: nonexistent.txt
complete_examples.ai_explanation: Try-with-resources (introduced in Java 7) is a major security improvement. The OLD way required 15+ lines just for cleanup in finally, and forgetting to close resources is a common bug that leads to resource exhaustion (another DoS vector). The NEW way automatically closes any resource declared in the try(...) parentheses when the block exits - whether normally or via exception. Resources must implement AutoCloseable. You can declare MULTIPLE resources separated by semicolons - they're all auto-closed in reverse order. This eliminates an entire category of resource leak vulnerabilities with cleaner, shorter code.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: What if the close() method itself throws an exception? Research 'suppressed exceptions' in Java - try-with-resources handles this elegantly.

complete_examples.title: Example 13: The throws Keyword — Declaring Exceptions
complete_examples.description: Shows how to use throws to declare exceptions and pass responsibility to callers
complete_examples.output:
=== safeRead handles it ===
Reading from: secrets.txt
Got: secret_data_here
Cannot read secrets - file missing

=== riskyRead passes it up ===
main() caught it: nonexistent.txt (No such file or directory)
complete_examples.ai_explanation: The throws keyword tells the compiler: 'This method might throw this exception, and I'm NOT handling it here.' readSecret() declares throws FileNotFoundException - anyone calling it MUST either catch it or declare it themselves. safeRead() CATCHES the exception (Option 1). riskyRead() DECLARES it with throws (Option 2), passing responsibility to ITS caller. Eventually, main() catches it. This is the 'hot potato' of exceptions - someone MUST eventually catch it, or the program crashes. Security implication: if you keep declaring throws all the way up without anyone catching, the exception reaches the JVM and could crash the application.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: What happens if main() doesn't catch the exception from riskyRead()? Try removing the try-catch in main and see what the JVM does. This is exactly how DoS attacks work!

complete_examples.title: Example 14: Complete Secure Application — Putting It All Together
complete_examples.description: A complete secure banking transfer system demonstrating all exception handling best practices
complete_examples.output:
=== Secure Bank Transfers ===
Starting balances: {ACC003=2500.0, ACC002=500.0, ACC001=1000.0}

Transfer of $200.00 completed. Ref: a1b2c3d4
Invalid amount format. Ref: e5f6g7h8
Transfer failed - account error. Ref: i9j0k1l2
Insufficient funds. Ref: m3n4o5p6
Transfer failed. Ref: q7r8s9t0
Transfer of $500.00 completed. Ref: u1v2w3x4

Final balances: {ACC003=2000.0, ACC002=700.0, ACC001=1300.0}

=== AUDIT LOG (Admin Only) ===
[a1b2c3d4] SUCCESS: ACC001 -> ACC002 $200.0
[e5f6g7h8] REJECTED: invalid amount format 'abc'
[i9j0k1l2] REJECTED: Account not found
[m3n4o5p6] REJECTED: Insufficient funds
[q7r8s9t0] REJECTED: null input
[u1v2w3x4] SUCCESS: ACC003 -> ACC001 $500.0
complete_examples.ai_explanation: This is a complete, production-quality secure system demonstrating ALL best practices: (1) INPUT VALIDATION before any processing - null checks, range checks, format checks. (2) CUSTOM EXCEPTIONS for domain-specific errors (InsufficientFundsException, AccountNotFoundException). (3) SPECIFIC CATCHES ordered from most specific to most general. (4) CATCH-ALL safety net at the end prevents any unexpected exception from crashing the system. (5) SECURE LOGGING with reference IDs - users see only generic messages. (6) AUDIT TRAIL records every action for security review. (7) FAIL-SAFE DEFAULTS - every error path returns false (transfer denied). Notice how the user NEVER sees account numbers, balances, or internal details in error messages - only the admin audit log has those.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Add transaction rollback! If the debit succeeds but the credit fails (add a simulated random failure), the debit should be reversed. This is why finally blocks matter in financial systems.

visual_diagram.title: Exception Handling & Security Vulnerabilities Flowchart
visual_diagram.content:
🛡️ EXCEPTION HANDLING & SECURITY FLOWCHART 🛡️

📋 PART 1: THE EXCEPTION HIERARCHY

🔴 Throwable (Root of ALL exceptions)
           │
     ┌─────┴─────┐
     │           │
❌ Error      ⚡ Exception
  (SERIOUS)       │
  │               ├──────────────────────┐
  │               │                      │
OutOfMemory   RuntimeException    Checked Exceptions
StackOverflow  (UNCHECKED ⚠️)          (MUST handle ✅)
              │                      │
        ┌─────┼─────┐         ┌─────┼─────┐
        │     │     │         │     │     │
      NPE  NFE  AIOOB    FNFE  IOE  SQLE

  NPE = NullPointerException    FNFE = FileNotFoundException
  NFE = NumberFormatException   IOE  = IOException
  AIOOB = ArrayIndexOutOfBounds  SQLE = SQLException

📋 PART 2: TRY-CATCH-FINALLY FLOW

START: Enter try block
         │
         ▼
   ┌────────────┐     No Exception
   │  Execute   │─────────────────────┐
   │  try code  │                     │
   └─────┬──────┘                     │
         │ Exception thrown!          │
         ▼                            │
   ┌────────────────┐                 │
   │ Match catch    │                 │
   │ block type?    │                 │
   └───┬────────┬───┘                 │
  YES │        │ NO                  │
       ▼        ▼                     │
Execute catch  Propagate up!       │
       │        │                     │
       ▼        ▼                     ▼
   ┌──────────────────────────────────────┐
   │  ⚡ FINALLY block ALWAYS executes ⚡    │
   │  • Close files / connections         │
   │  • Release locks                      │
   │  • Clean up temporary data            │
   └──────────────────────────────────────┘
         │
         ▼
Program continues (if caught)

📋 PART 3: SECURITY VULNERABILITY — AUTH BYPASS

🔴 VULNERABLE LOGIN FLOW

1. boolean loggedIn = TRUE  ← ⚠️ DANGER: Default is permissive!
         │
         ▼
2. try { check password }
         │
    ┌────┴────┐
    │         │
  Match    Exception! (e.g. null password)
    │         │
    ▼         ▼
loggedIn  catch { /* empty! */ }  ← Does NOTHING!
= false       │
    │         │
    └────┬────┘
         ▼
3. return loggedIn  → Returns TRUE → ATTACKER GETS IN! 🔓


✅ SECURE LOGIN FLOW

1. boolean loggedIn = FALSE  ← ✅ SAFE: Default denies access
         │
         ▼
2. Null check inputs first  ← ✅ Prevents NPE attack
         │
         ▼
3. try { check password }
         │
    ┌────┴────┐
    │         │
  Match    Exception!
    │         │
    ▼         ▼
loggedIn  catch { loggedIn = false; log(); }  ← ✅ Explicit deny!
= true        │
    │         │
    └────┬────┘
         ▼
4. return loggedIn  → Only TRUE if password verified ✅

📋 PART 4: INFORMATION LEAKAGE COMPARISON

❌ VULNERABLE Error Message (attacker sees):
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ Error: Wrong password for user=admin                │
│ entered=hacker123 expected=SuperSecret123            │
│ at LoginServlet.validate(LoginServlet.java:42)      │
│ at LoginServlet.doPost(LoginServlet.java:15)        │
│ at javax.servlet.http.HttpServlet.service(...)      │
│ Database: jdbc:mysql://internal-db:3306/users        │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
  ⚠️ Leaks: real password, class names, DB connection, line numbers!

✅ SECURE Error Message (attacker sees):
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ Login failed. Username or password is incorrect.    │
│ Contact support with reference: ERR-7f2a             │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
  ✅ Reveals NOTHING useful to attacker!

🔒 ADMIN LOG (only authorised staff see):
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ [ERR-7f2a] Auth failure user=admin                  │
│ Exception: BadPasswordException at line 42          │
│ IP: 192.168.1.50 Time: 2026-02-21 14:30:01        │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
  🔒 Full details for debugging, access-controlled

📋 PART 5: 10 RULES FOR SECURE EXCEPTION HANDLING

RULE 1  Use fail-safe defaults (deny by default)
RULE 2  Catch specific exceptions before general ones
RULE 3  NEVER leave catch blocks empty
RULE 4  Don't use exceptions for normal flow control
RULE 5  Don't expose internal details in error messages
RULE 6  Log sensitive details to secure logs only
RULE 7  Use finally for resource cleanup
RULE 8  Validate inputs BEFORE processing
RULE 9  Don't log stack traces or config data publicly
RULE 10 Add catch-all handlers for long-lived services

These 10 rules prevent the three main exception attacks:
🔓 Authentication Bypass — Rules 1, 3, 8
📢 Information Leakage  — Rules 5, 6, 9
💥 Denial of Service    — Rules 7, 10

💡 REMEMBER!

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  An EMPTY catch block is more dangerous than NO catch.   │
│  It gives you a FALSE sense of security while leaving   │
│  the door wide open for attackers!                      │
│                                                         │
│  The Ariane 5 rocket explosion (1996) was caused by     │
│  an unhandled exception — $370 million lost in 37 sec!  │
│                                                         │
│  CWE-395: Don't catch NPE to detect null — check first! │
│  CWE-476: Always validate references before using them   │
│  CWE-200: Never reveal internal details in error msgs    │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 1: Your First Try-Catch
practice_exercises.description: Write a try-catch block that catches an ArithmeticException when dividing by zero and prints a friendly error message.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Let's start with the most basic exception! Division by zero is like trying to split a pizza into zero slices - it doesn't make sense. Let's catch that error!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Error: Cannot divide by zero!
Program continues safely!

hints.message: Wrap the division line inside a try { } block, then add a catch (ArithmeticException e) { } after it.
Move the int result = numerator / denominator; line INSIDE the try block. Put your error message inside the catch block.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 2: Catching NullPointerException
practice_exercises.description: Fix the code that crashes when trying to get the length of a null String. Use try-catch to handle the NullPointerException.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: NullPointerException is the most common exception in Java! It happens when you try to use a variable that points to... nothing. Let's learn to catch it!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Name is not set!
Program handled the null safely!

hints.message: Put the name.length() call inside a try block, then catch NullPointerException.
try { int length = name.length(); ... } catch (NullPointerException e) { System.out.println("Name is not set!"); }

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 3: Multiple Catch Blocks
practice_exercises.description: Write code that catches both NumberFormatException and ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException with separate messages.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Different problems need different solutions! Just like a doctor diagnoses specific illnesses, your code should catch specific exceptions with specific messages.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Cannot convert 'abc' to a number!
Index out of range!

hints.message: You need TWO separate try-catch blocks - one for each test. Each catch handles a different exception type.
First try-catch: catch (NumberFormatException e) for the parseInt. Second try-catch: catch (ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException e) for the array access.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 4: The Finally Block
practice_exercises.description: Add a finally block that prints 'Cleanup complete!' after a try-catch, regardless of whether an exception occurred.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: The finally block is like a cleanup crew - it ALWAYS shows up, whether things went well or badly. Let's see it in action!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
=== Test 1: No exception ===
Result: 5
Cleanup complete!

=== Test 2: Exception ===
Cannot divide by zero
Cleanup complete!

hints.message: Add the finally { } block AFTER the catch block. It will run in both test cases.
try { int result = a/b; ... } catch (ArithmeticException e) { ... } finally { System.out.println("Cleanup complete!"); }

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 5: Predict the Output — Exception Flow
practice_exercises.description: Read the code carefully and predict what will be printed. Think about which lines execute and which are skipped.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Time to think like a computer! Trace through this code step by step and predict what gets printed. Remember: once an exception is thrown, the rest of the try block is SKIPPED!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
A
B
D
E
F

hints.message: Line 'A' prints first (before try). 'B' prints inside try. Then exception occurs at 10/0. What happens to 'C'?
'C' is SKIPPED because the exception jumps to the catch block. 'D' prints in catch. 'E' ALWAYS prints in finally. Does 'F' print?
Output is: A, B, D, E, F. 'C' is skipped because the exception at 10/0 causes execution to jump directly to the catch block. finally always runs, and F prints after the entire try-catch-finally.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 6: Checked vs Unchecked — Identify the Type
practice_exercises.description: Classify each exception as checked or unchecked and explain whether the compiler forces you to handle it.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Let's sort exceptions into two teams: checked (compiler forces handling) and unchecked (compiler lets you ignore them). Remember: rUNtime = UNchecked!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
1. UNCHECKED (extends RuntimeException)
2. CHECKED (extends IOException extends Exception)
3. UNCHECKED (extends RuntimeException)
4. CHECKED (extends Exception directly)
5. UNCHECKED (extends RuntimeException)
6. CHECKED (extends Exception directly)
7. UNCHECKED (extends RuntimeException)
8. CHECKED (extends Exception directly)
Rule: If it extends RuntimeException = UNCHECKED. Otherwise = CHECKED.

hints.message: The key question: does it extend RuntimeException? If yes → UNCHECKED. If it extends Exception but NOT RuntimeException → CHECKED.
NullPointerException, ArithmeticException, ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException, NumberFormatException are all RuntimeException subclasses → UNCHECKED.
UNCHECKED: 1, 3, 5, 7 (all RuntimeException). CHECKED: 2, 4, 6, 8 (extend Exception, not RuntimeException). Rule: rUNtime = UNchecked!

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 7: Throwing Your Own Exception
practice_exercises.description: Write a method that validates age and throws IllegalArgumentException if the age is negative or over 150.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Sometimes YOUR code needs to signal a problem! The 'throw' keyword lets you create and throw exceptions when something doesn't look right.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Valid age: 25
Error: Age cannot be negative
Error: Age cannot exceed 150

hints.message: Use 'throw new IllegalArgumentException("message");' to throw an exception with a custom message.
if (age < 0) { throw new IllegalArgumentException("Age cannot be negative"); } else if (age > 150) { ... }

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 8: The throws Declaration
practice_exercises.description: Write a method that declares it throws FileNotFoundException, and a caller that handles it.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Sometimes you want to say 'I know this could go wrong, but I want my CALLER to deal with it.' That's what throws does - it passes the responsibility up!
practice_exercises.expected_output: File not found!

hints.message: Add 'throws FileNotFoundException' after the method name: static void openFile(String filename) throws FileNotFoundException
In main, wrap the call in try-catch: try { openFile("secret_data.txt"); } catch (FileNotFoundException e) { ... }

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 9: Exception Message Methods
practice_exercises.description: Use getMessage(), getClass().getSimpleName(), and toString() to extract information from an exception object.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Exception objects carry useful information! Let's learn the three ways to get details from an exception - but remember, in production you'd only show these in secure logs!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Message: Index 10 out of bounds for length 3
Type: ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException
Full: java.lang.ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException: Index 10 out of bounds for length 3

hints.message: System.out.println("Message: " + e.getMessage()); will print just the error message text.
e.getClass().getSimpleName() gives you just the class name without the package prefix.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 10: Fix the Crash — NumberFormatException
practice_exercises.description: Fix the user input processing code that crashes when given non-numeric input.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Users type all sorts of crazy things into input fields! Your job is to make sure the program handles it gracefully instead of crashing.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Valid age: 25
Invalid input: abc
Invalid input: null
Invalid input: -5
Invalid input: 200
Valid age: 42

hints.message: First check if input is null (before trying to parse it). Then wrap parseInt in try-catch for NumberFormatException.
if (input == null) return -1; then try { int age = Integer.parseInt(input); if (age < 0 || age > 150) return -1; return age; } catch (NumberFormatException e) { return -1; }

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 11: Spot the Bug — Login Bypass
practice_exercises.description: Read the login code and identify the security vulnerability that allows authentication bypass.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: This is the EXACT bug that has caused real data breaches! Can you spot why an attacker could log in without knowing the password?
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Normal: false
Attack: true

Vulnerability: loggedIn starts as TRUE (should be FALSE)
Exploit: Pass null as password -> NullPointerException at pwd.equals()
Empty catch block does nothing -> loggedIn stays TRUE -> access granted!
Fix: (1) Set loggedIn = false, (2) Add null checks, (3) Set loggedIn = false in catch

hints.message: Look at line 2: what is the initial value of loggedIn? What happens if an exception is thrown before it's set to false?
If pwd is null, pwd.equals(realPwd) throws NullPointerException. The empty catch block does nothing. loggedIn is still true from line 2!
Three fixes needed: (1) boolean loggedIn = false; (2) if (pwd == null) return false; (3) catch block: loggedIn = false;

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 12: Fix the Login — Secure Authentication
practice_exercises.description: Rewrite the vulnerable login method to be secure against the null password attack.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Now that you've spotted the bug, let's fix it! Apply all three security fixes to make this login bulletproof.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Valid login: true
Wrong pwd: false
Null pwd: false
Null user: false
Both null: false

hints.message: Start with boolean loggedIn = false; and add: if (user == null || pwd == null) return false;
In the try block, only set loggedIn = true when the password matches. In catch, explicitly set loggedIn = false.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 13: Empty Catch Block Detector
practice_exercises.description: Review code samples and identify which catch blocks are dangerously empty.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Empty catch blocks are like leaving your front door open! Let's train your eye to spot them - security auditors always check for these.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
method1: DANGEROUS - empty catch, silently swallows error
method2: SAFE - provides user feedback
method3: NEEDS IMPROVEMENT - prints stack trace (info leakage!) and valid stays true on error
method4: SAFE - sets fail-safe default and logs securely

hints.message: An empty catch block (or one with just a comment) is ALWAYS dangerous. It silently hides errors.
Printing stack traces with e.printStackTrace() leaks internal information. Also check: does the boolean stay in a safe state on error?
method1: DANGEROUS (empty catch). method2: SAFE. method3: NEEDS IMPROVEMENT (stack trace leaks info + valid stays true). method4: SAFE (fail-safe + secure logging).

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 14: Default Value Attack
practice_exercises.description: Create a method vulnerable to the 'default value' attack, then fix it.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: The 'default value' attack exploits code that starts with a permissive default. Let's see how dangerous this pattern is across different scenarios!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
=== Vulnerable ===
Valid token: true
Null attack: true
=== Secure ===
Valid token: true
Null attack: false

hints.message: The secure version should start with isAdmin = false and check for null before comparing.
if (token == null) return false; Then only set isAdmin = true if the token matches exactly.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 15: Fail-Safe vs Fail-Open
practice_exercises.description: Classify code examples as 'fail-safe' (secure on error) or 'fail-open' (insecure on error).
practice_exercises.ai_intro: In security, there are two ways to handle failure: fail-SAFE (lock everything down) or fail-OPEN (let everything through). One of these is ALWAYS wrong in security!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
methodA: FAIL-SAFE - default is false (deny), stays safe on exception
methodB: FAIL-OPEN - default is true (allow), empty catch means error = access granted
methodC: FAIL-OPEN - catch returns true, any error grants access
methodD: FAIL-SAFE - catch returns false, any error denies access

hints.message: Ask yourself: if an exception is thrown, does the user get ACCESS (fail-open) or get DENIED (fail-safe)?
Check the default boolean value AND what the catch block does (or doesn't do).
A: FAIL-SAFE (false default, stays false on error). B: FAIL-OPEN (true default, empty catch). C: FAIL-OPEN (catch returns true). D: FAIL-SAFE (catch returns false).

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 16: Build a Secure PIN Checker
practice_exercises.description: Create a PIN validation method that is immune to null attacks and has proper error handling.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Banks use PIN codes to protect accounts. Let's build a PIN checker that an attacker can't bypass with null tricks!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Correct PIN: true
Wrong PIN: false
[LOG] Failed PIN attempt: null input
Null attack: false
[LOG] Failed PIN attempt: empty input
Empty: false
[LOG] Failed PIN attempt: wrong length
Too short: false
[LOG] Failed PIN attempt: non-numeric
Letters: false
[LOG] Failed PIN attempt: wrong length
SQL inject: false

hints.message: Start with null and empty checks. Then check length == 4. Then check all characters are digits. Finally compare with correctPin.
Use pin.matches("\\d{4}") to check if it's exactly 4 digits. Or loop through chars and check Character.isDigit().

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 17: Authentication Bypass — Multiple Attack Vectors
practice_exercises.description: Test a login system with various attack inputs and determine which ones succeed.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Attackers don't just try null! They try empty strings, special characters, very long strings, and more. Let's test all the attack vectors!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
=== Testing Secure Login ===
user=admin    pwd=admin123        -> true
user=admin    pwd=null            -> false
user=null     pwd=admin123        -> false
user=null     pwd=null            -> false
user=admin    pwd=                -> false
user=         pwd=admin123        -> false
user=admin    pwd=' OR '1'='1     -> false

hints.message: Check for null first, then check for empty strings, then do the actual comparison.
if (user == null || pwd == null || user.isEmpty() || pwd.isEmpty()) return false;

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 18: Trace the Attack — Step by Step
practice_exercises.description: Trace through the vulnerable code line by line showing variable values as an attacker bypasses authentication.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Security experts trace code execution step by step to find vulnerabilities. Let's do exactly that - follow the code like a debugger!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Line 1: loggedIn = true (set to true - the vulnerability!)
Line 2: realPwd = "secret" (stored password)
Line 3: Enter try block
Line 4: pwd.toUpperCase() called but pwd is NULL -> NullPointerException thrown!
Line 5: SKIPPED (exception already thrown)
Line 6: SKIPPED (never reaches this)
Line 7: catch(Exception e) catches the NullPointerException
Line 8: Empty catch - does NOTHING
Line 9: loggedIn is still TRUE -> returns TRUE -> ATTACKER GETS IN!

hints.message: At Line 4, pwd is null. What happens when you call null.toUpperCase()? What exception is thrown?
NullPointerException at Line 4 means Lines 5 and 6 are SKIPPED. The catch block on Line 7-8 does nothing. What is loggedIn still?
The complete trace shows: loggedIn starts as true, NPE thrown at line 4, empty catch does nothing, loggedIn is STILL true, returns true. Attack succeeds!

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 19: Role-Based Access with Exception Safety
practice_exercises.description: Build a role checker that safely handles exceptions and denies access by default.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: In real systems, different users have different permissions. Let's build a role checker that's safe even when things go wrong!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Admin role: 3
User role: 1
Null role: 0
Empty role: 0

Admin access (alice): true
Admin access (bob): false
Admin access (null): false

hints.message: Check for null and empty inputs first, returning 0 (GUEST level). Use a switch or if-else on the role string.
switch(role.toUpperCase()) { case "ADMIN": return 3; case "MODERATOR": return 2; case "USER": return 1; default: return 0; }

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 20: Build an Unbreakable Authenticator
practice_exercises.description: Create a complete authentication class with rate limiting, null safety, and secure logging.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Final boss of authentication! Build a complete, production-quality authenticator that handles EVERY attack vector we've learned about.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
SUCCESS: Welcome admin
DENIED: Invalid credentials
DENIED: Invalid input
DENIED: Invalid input
DENIED: Invalid credentials
DENIED: Invalid credentials
DENIED: Account locked
DENIED: Account locked

hints.message: Start with input validation (null/empty checks), then rate limiting (check failedAttempts map), then credential verification.
Use failedAttempts.getOrDefault(user, 0) to check attempt count. Always use the SAME error message for wrong user and wrong password.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 21: Spot the Information Leak
practice_exercises.description: Identify what sensitive information the error message reveals to an attacker.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Error messages can be goldmines for attackers! Let's train your eye to spot when code reveals too much.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Error 1 leaks: table name (USERS), that admin exists as a user
Error 2 leaks: database IP, port, database name, database type (MySQL)
Error 3 leaks: server file system path, config file location
Error 4 leaks: username, attempted password, AND the real password!
Error 5 leaks: class name (LoginServlet), technology (Java), exact line number

Secure versions should ALL say something like:
"An error occurred. Please try again or contact support with reference: ERR-XXXXX"

hints.message: For each error, ask: what could an attacker LEARN from this that helps them attack further?
Error 1: table name + user exists. Error 2: DB location. Error 3: file path. Error 4: THE ACTUAL PASSWORD. Error 5: code structure.
Replace ALL with generic messages like 'An error occurred. Reference: ERR-XXXXX' and log details privately.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 22: Build a Secure Error Handler
practice_exercises.description: Create a method that converts detailed exceptions into safe user-facing messages with reference IDs.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Let's build the error handler that every secure application needs - it hides the details from users while preserving them for admins!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
An error occurred. Reference: ERR-XXXXX
An error occurred. Reference: ERR-YYYYY

--- Admin Log ---
[ERR-XXXXX] Context: parsing | NumberFormatException: For input string: "abc"
[ERR-YYYYY] Context: processing | NullPointerException: null

hints.message: Use 'ERR-' + (int)(Math.random()*90000+10000) to generate a reference ID.
adminLog.add('[' + refId + '] Context: ' + context + ' | ' + e.getClass().getSimpleName() + ': ' + e.getMessage());

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 23: Stack Trace Sanitiser
practice_exercises.description: Write a method that extracts useful information from a stack trace without exposing internal details.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Stack traces are incredibly useful for debugging but incredibly dangerous if exposed. Let's build a sanitiser!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Data error
Input format error
Data range error
Processing error

hints.message: Use instanceof to check the exception type: if (e instanceof NullPointerException) return "Data error";
Chain if-else with instanceof checks for each known type, with a default at the end.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 24: Username Enumeration Prevention
practice_exercises.description: Fix login code that reveals whether a username exists through different error messages.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Attackers use different error messages to figure out which usernames exist! This is called 'username enumeration' and it's a real vulnerability.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
=== Vulnerable ===
Error: Wrong password for user 'admin'
Error: Username 'hacker' does not exist
=== Secure ===
Invalid username or password
Invalid username or password

hints.message: Use the SAME error message whether the username is wrong or the password is wrong. The attacker shouldn't be able to tell the difference.
Check both conditions but return the same string: "Invalid username or password"

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 25: Rewrite Verbose Error Messages
practice_exercises.description: Transform 5 verbose error messages into secure versions.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Let's practice the art of writing error messages that are helpful to users but useless to attackers!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
SECURE 1: A service is temporarily unavailable. Please try again.
SECURE 2: File upload failed. Please check the file and try again.
SECURE 3: Your session has expired. Please log in again.
SECURE 4: Payment could not be processed. Please check your details.
SECURE 5: You do not have permission for this action.

hints.message: Remove ALL internal details: no IPs, no paths, no user IDs, no tokens, no class names, no line numbers.
Give the user enough info to know what happened and what to do, but nothing that helps an attacker.
1: 'Service temporarily unavailable'. 2: 'File upload failed, check file'. 3: 'Session expired, please log in'. 4: 'Payment could not be processed'. 5: 'Permission denied'.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 26: Secure vs Insecure Exception Reporting
practice_exercises.description: Compare two server implementations and identify which error responses are secure.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Web servers often expose too much info in error responses. Let's compare secure and insecure approaches!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
A: INSECURE - exposes DB table name, DAO class, servlet, line numbers
B: SECURE - generic message with unpredictable reference ID
C: SECURE - helpful for user, reveals no internal details
D: INSECURE - confirms user 'admin' exists, reveals API structure

hints.message: Ask: does this response reveal internal architecture, class names, file paths, or database structure?
A leaks everything. B is ideal. C is good (validation feedback is OK). D reveals too much about the user and API.
A: INSECURE (DB table, classes, lines). B: SECURE (generic + ref). C: SECURE (helpful, no internals). D: INSECURE (confirms user, reveals API path).

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 27: Build a Production Error Page
practice_exercises.description: Create a method that generates appropriate error responses for different HTTP status codes.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Real web applications need different error pages for different situations. Let's build error responses that are helpful but secure!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
400 Bad Request: Please check your input and try again.
401 Unauthorized: Please log in to continue.
403 Forbidden: You do not have permission for this action.
404 Not Found: The requested page could not be found.
500 Internal Error: Something went wrong. Reference: ERR-XXXXX

hints.message: Use a switch statement on statusCode. For 500 errors, generate a reference ID. NEVER include the exception message!
switch(statusCode) { case 400: return "400..."; case 500: String ref = ...; return "500... Ref: " + ref; }

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 28: Log Sanitisation — Remove Sensitive Data
practice_exercises.description: Write a method that sanitises log messages to remove passwords, emails, and card numbers.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Even admin logs shouldn't contain raw passwords or card numbers! Let's build a sanitiser for secure logging.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Login failed: user=admin pwd=****
Payment with card ************1111 failed
User j***@company.com logged in

hints.message: Use String.replaceAll() with regex patterns. For passwords: replaceAll("pwd=[^ ]*", "pwd=****")
For cards: find 16-digit sequences and keep only last 4. For emails: find the @ symbol and mask the local part.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 29: Timing Attack via Exception Messages
practice_exercises.description: Explain how different response times for errors can leak information.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Even the TIME it takes to return an error can reveal secrets! This is called a timing side-channel attack.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Both return 'Invalid credentials' but vulnerable version returns faster for non-existent users.
Attacker can time thousands of requests to determine which usernames exist.

Fix: Always perform the password comparison even if user doesn't exist.
Use a dummy hash comparison for non-existent users to equalise timing.

hints.message: The problem: even though both return the same MESSAGE, one returns faster. An attacker can measure the time difference over many requests.
Fix: always perform a password comparison. If user doesn't exist, compare against a dummy value to waste the same amount of time.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 30: Complete Secure Error Handling System
practice_exercises.description: Build a complete error handling system with severity levels, secure logging, and user notifications.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Let's build the complete error handling system that a real production application would use!
practice_exercises.expected_output: [User sees generic messages, admin sees full details in log]

hints.message: Use instanceof to determine severity. Generate refId with Math.random(). Switch on severity for user message.
Severity s = (e instanceof SecurityException) ? CRITICAL : (e instanceof NullPointerException) ? HIGH : ...

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 31: Server Crash Prevention
practice_exercises.description: Fix a server loop that crashes on the first bad request.

Processed: 42
[400] Invalid request: abc
[400] Invalid request: null
[400] Invalid request: (empty)
Processed: 99
Server still running!

hints.message: Wrap everything in try-catch. Check for null and empty first.
if (input == null || input.isEmpty()) { print error; return; } try { parseInt... } catch (NumberFormatException) { print error; }

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 32: Resource Cleanup with Finally
practice_exercises.description: Fix code that leaks database connections when exceptions occur.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Leaked database connections are like leaving taps running - eventually you run out of water (connections) and everything stops!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Query OK on conn_1
Vulnerable - Open connections: 1
Query OK on conn_1
[BLOCKED] Dangerous SQL detected
Secure - Open connections: 0

hints.message: Use finally { if (conn != null) closeConnection(conn); } to ensure cleanup.
try { conn = openConnection(); if (sql.contains("DROP")) throw...; } catch { print error; } finally { close; }

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 33: Try-With-Resources Practice
practice_exercises.description: Convert old-style try-finally code to modern try-with-resources.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Java 7 introduced try-with-resources to make cleanup automatic. Let's practice this cleaner approach!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Old: Hello secure world!
New: Hello secure world!
Missing: Error: missing.txt (No such file or directory)

hints.message: Put the resource declaration in parentheses: try (BufferedReader br = new BufferedReader(new FileReader(filename))) { }
The resource auto-closes! No need for finally block at all.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 34: Exception-Based DoS Simulation
practice_exercises.description: Simulate a DoS attack and build defences against it.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: An attacker sends thousands of requests designed to crash your server. Let's simulate this and build defences!
practice_exercises.expected_output: [All 15 requests handled, server stays running]

hints.message: Wrap everything in try-catch(Exception). Count served++ on success and crashCount++ on any error.
Validate input (null, empty, range). Parse in try-catch. Count everything.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 35: Catch-All Strategy for Services
practice_exercises.description: Implement the layered catch strategy: specific → general → catch-all.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Good exception handling works in layers, like a building's security: front door, lobby guard, then alarm system. Let's build all three layers!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
5 -> Result: 20
0 -> Error: Cannot divide by zero
null -> Error: Input cannot be null
abc -> Error: Not a valid number
-1 -> Result: -100
20 -> Result: 5

hints.message: Check null first, then try parseInt and division in a try block with multiple catch blocks.
catch (NumberFormatException) for bad numbers, catch (ArithmeticException) for divide by zero, catch (Exception) for everything else.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 36: Request Validator with Exception Safety
practice_exercises.description: Build an input validator that rejects bad data before it can cause exceptions.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Prevention is better than cure! Let's validate inputs BEFORE they reach code that could throw exceptions.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
ACCEPTED: user=123
REJECTED: Missing user ID
REJECTED: User ID must be numeric
REJECTED: Age out of range
REJECTED: Invalid email format

hints.message: Check each field in order: null/empty → format → range. Return the first error found.
if (userId==null||userId.isEmpty()) return "Missing user ID"; try { Integer.parseInt(userId); } catch { return "not numeric"; }

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 37: Connection Pool Exhaustion
practice_exercises.description: Simulate and fix a resource leak that exhausts a connection pool.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: When all connections leak, nobody can connect — the server becomes unusable even though it's running. A slow death!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
=== Vulnerable ===
POOL EXHAUSTED!
POOL EXHAUSTED!
Connections left: 0

=== Secure ===
[All connections properly released]
Connections left: 3

hints.message: Use try-finally to ensure releaseConnection() is always called.
boolean connected = getConnection(); try { ... } finally { if (connected) releaseConnection(); }

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 38: Graceful Degradation
practice_exercises.description: Build a service that falls back to cached data when the primary data source throws exceptions.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: When one system fails, a good service doesn't just crash — it falls back to a backup plan!

[Source: cache] CACHED: user_profile
[Source: default] DEFAULT: No data available
[Source: primary] LIVE: user_profile

hints.message: Try primary in a try block. In catch, try cache. If cache fails too, return default.
try { return fetchFromPrimary(key); } catch { try { return fetchFromCache(key); } catch { return fetchDefault(); } }

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 39: Circuit Breaker Pattern
practice_exercises.description: Implement a simple circuit breaker that stops calling a failing service after too many errors.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: A circuit breaker prevents your system from repeatedly hammering a dead service — just like an electrical circuit breaker prevents fires!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Call 1: ERROR: Service unavailable (1/3)
Call 2: ERROR: Service unavailable (2/3)
Call 3: ERROR: Service unavailable (3/3) - CIRCUIT OPENED
Call 4: CIRCUIT OPEN: Service temporarily unavailable
Call 5: CIRCUIT OPEN: Service temporarily unavailable
Call 6: CIRCUIT OPEN: Service temporarily unavailable

hints.message: Check isOpen first. Then try calling the service. On exception, increment failCount and check threshold.
if (isOpen) return "CIRCUIT OPEN..."; try { ... } catch { failCount++; if (failCount >= threshold) isOpen = true; }

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 40: Resilient Batch Processor
practice_exercises.description: Build a batch processor that continues processing even when individual items fail.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: When processing 100 items, don't let one bad item stop the other 99! Let's build a resilient batch processor.
practice_exercises.expected_output:
HELLO
[Error] Item 2: NullPointerException
WORLD
[Error] Item 4: IllegalArgumentException
[Error] Item 5: RuntimeException
JAVA
TEST
Success: 4 Errors: 3

hints.message: Loop through items, wrapping each processItem call in its own try-catch.
for (int i=0; i<items.length; i++) { try { String r = processItem(items[i]); println(r); success++; } catch (Exception e) { println error; errors++; } }

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 41: CWE-395 — Detect the Anti-Pattern
practice_exercises.description: Find code that uses NullPointerException catch to detect null instead of checking properly.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: CWE-395 is a real vulnerability catalogued by MITRE. Let's learn to spot it!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
A: CWE-395 (catches NPE instead of checking null)
B: CORRECT (explicit null check)
C: CWE-395 (catches NPE for null array instead of checking)

hints.message: CWE-395 = catching NullPointerException to handle null. The fix: check for null BEFORE using the variable.
methodA catches NPE for null name. methodC catches NPE for null array. Both should use if (x == null) instead.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 42: CWE-476 — Null Pointer Dereference Chain
practice_exercises.description: Fix a chain of method calls that dereferences potentially null values.

Alice: LONDON
Bob: UNKNOWN
Null: UNKNOWN

hints.message: Check each step: if (user == null) return "UNKNOWN"; if (user.profile == null) return "UNKNOWN"; etc.
You need 4 null checks: user, user.profile, user.profile.address, user.profile.address.city

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 43: CWE-200 — Sensitive Data in Exceptions
practice_exercises.description: Fix code that exposes sensitive data through exception messages.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: CWE-200 is about leaking sensitive info. Exception messages are a common way this happens!

Secure: Transfer exceeds the allowed limit. Please contact support.

hints.message: The secure version should throw an exception with a generic message - no account numbers, no balances.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 44: Custom Security Exception
practice_exercises.description: Create a custom exception class that stores audit info separately from the user message.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 45: Exception Hierarchy Design
practice_exercises.description: Design a hierarchy of custom exceptions for a banking application.

Each subclass calls super("user message") and stores audit details in a field.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 46: Predict the Exception Type
practice_exercises.description: For each code snippet, predict which exception will be thrown.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 47: Secure File Reader with Exception Handling
practice_exercises.description: Build a file reader that handles all possible file-related exceptions securely.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 48: Exception-Safe Configuration Loader
practice_exercises.description: Build a config loader with fallback defaults when config files are missing.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 49: Multi-Catch and Exception Chaining
practice_exercises.description: Use Java 7+ multi-catch syntax and exception chaining.

Input error: / by zero
Result: 20

hints.message: Use: catch (NumberFormatException | ArithmeticException e) { ... } - one catch handles both!

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 50: Exception Handling Code Review
practice_exercises.description: Review a complete class and list all security issues found.

{"status": 500, "message": "Internal server error", "reference": "REF-YYYYY"}

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 59: Build a Vulnerability Scanner

practice_exercises.description: Build a complete, production-quality request handler implementing ALL security best practices.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: This is the FINAL BOSS! Combine everything you've learned into one complete, secure request handler. You've got this!

[200] Welcome, admin
[401] Invalid username or password. Ref: XXX
